Ultramarine blue was historically more expensive than gold — it was ground from lapis lazuli imported from Afghanistan, and Renaissance painters often reserved it exclusively for depicting the Virgin Mary's robes.
Tyrian purple, worn by Roman emperors, required the harvested glands of approximately 10,000 murex sea snails to produce a single gram of dye.
The color mauve was discovered by accident in 1856 when 18-year-old chemist William Perkin was trying to synthesize quinine and instead produced the world's first synthetic textile dye.
Flamingos are born with grey feathers and turn pink from carotenoid pigments in the algae and crustaceans they eat — captive flamingos must be fed synthetic carotenoids to maintain their coloration.
Polar bear fur is not white — each individual hair shaft is transparent and hollow, and the apparent white color results from light scattering within the structure.
The blue morpho butterfly's wings contain no blue pigment — the iridescent color comes entirely from microscopic surface structures that interfere with specific wavelengths of light.

Isaac Newton was the first to arrange colors in a circle, bending the visible spectrum into a loop in 1666 specifically to show that red and violet — the two ends — were visually related to each other.
The ancient Egyptians invented the first known synthetic pigment — Egyptian Blue — around 2,600 BC, and it emits a faint glow under near-infrared light that is invisible to the naked eye.
The English word for the color orange didn't exist until the 15th century, when the fruit arrived in Europe — before that, the color was typically described as 'yellow-red' or 'saffron.'

Russian has two separate basic words for blue — 'goluboy' for light blue and 'siniy' for dark blue — and studies show Russian speakers distinguish these shades a measurable fraction of a second faster than English speakers.

Japanese traffic lights appear green but were officially designated as 'ao' (青), a word historically meaning both blue and green — Japan eventually shifted to a blue-tinted green specifically to justify the classification.
The pigment known as 'mummy brown' was made from ground Egyptian mummies and was used by European painters as a glazing medium from the 1600s until the early 20th century.
French artist Yves Klein patented a proprietary shade of ultramarine in 1960 — International Klein Blue — and the specific binding formula has never been publicly disclosed.

Research analyzing 2004 Olympic combat sports found that athletes assigned the color red won a statistically significant higher proportion of close bouts than those assigned blue, even after controlling for skill level.
The Statue of Liberty's green patina took approximately 30 years to develop fully after the copper structure was installed in 1886 — it appeared bright copper-orange for most of its first decade.

A shade called 'Baker-Miller Pink' was tested in US correctional facilities in the 1980s and claimed to reduce violent incidents, though subsequent research found the calming effect lasted only about 15 minutes of exposure before dissipating.
Van Gogh's signature yellows have darkened significantly in many paintings because the lead chromate pigment he used was chemically unstable — the problem was documented even during his own lifetime.

Facebook's signature blue is partly attributed to Mark Zuckerberg's red-green color blindness — he has stated that blue is the color he perceives most vividly and that this influenced the platform's early interface design.
Tiffany & Co.'s robin's egg blue (Pantone 1837) is named after the year the company was founded and has been trademarked as a distinctive trade dress color since the 1990s.

UPS specifically chose brown for its trucks and uniforms after research suggested the color projected professionalism and cleanliness — the campaign slogan 'What can Brown do for you?' leaned directly into this association.
Chinese emperors reserved yellow exclusively for imperial use — unauthorized wearing of imperial yellow was a capital offense punishable by death during the Ming and Qing dynasties.

Queen Victoria's choice to wear white at her 1840 wedding was a deliberate statement of wealth and status, not a religious requirement — it established the Western tradition of white bridal gowns almost single-handedly.

Isaac Newton identified indigo as a separate color in the visible spectrum primarily to give it seven colors, mirroring the seven notes of the musical scale — many color scientists today argue indigo is not perceptually distinct from blue and violet.

The mantis shrimp has 16 types of color photoreceptors compared to 3 in humans, but research published in 2014 suggests the animal may not perceive more colors — instead it appears to process color information using a rapid parallel system rather than comparison.

Vantablack, developed in 2014, absorbs approximately 99.965% of visible light and was briefly licensed exclusively to artist Anish Kapoor, sparking a highly public feud with other artists denied access to the material.
Studies suggest that hospital patients whose rooms contain blue-green tones or views of nature request measurably less pain medication on average than patients in identical white-walled rooms.
The color chartreuse is named after a French liqueur made by Carthusian monks at Grande Chartreuse monastery since 1737 — the color was named after the drink, which was named after the monastery.
Red-green color blindness affects approximately 8% of men and only 0.5% of women, because the genes encoding red and green photoreceptors are both located on the X chromosome.

Studies suggest that people in red-painted rooms perceive time as passing more quickly than those in blue rooms — an effect some casinos have allegedly exploited to encourage guests to stay at tables longer.

The combination of red and yellow used by McDonald's was chosen because yellow activates hunger responses and red creates urgency — together they are designed to maximize impulse visits from passing drivers.

The Impressionist movement in painting was partly enabled by the 1841 invention of portable tin paint tubes, which allowed artists to work outdoors with pre-mixed colors for the first time in history.
The word 'crimson' derives from the Arabic 'qirmiz,' referring to the kermes scale insect from which the dye was traditionally made — the same insect whose Latin name gives us 'carmine.'
Studies suggest that people unconsciously rate food as tasting sweeter when served on red or pink plates rather than white ones, due to associations between red tones and the ripeness of fruit.
Red light is absorbed within the first 10 meters of seawater — at depths below 30 meters, red-colored fish appear grey or black because there is no remaining red light to reflect off them.

Brazil's national flag color — a legally defined 'verde bandeira' — is specified in federal law with precise spectrophotometric measurements, making it one of the most precisely defined flag colors in the world.
The green of John Deere farm equipment was trademarked as early as 1876, making it one of the longest-running continuous corporate color trademarks in American history.

Blue streetlights were installed in parts of Glasgow and certain neighborhoods in Japan in the 2000s after pilot studies reported reductions in nighttime crime — though researchers disagree on whether color or simply increased brightness was the key variable.

Synthetic indigo was invented by Adolf von Baeyer in 1897 — within 15 years, it had almost entirely displaced the natural indigo-farming industry that had sustained rural economies across South Carolina and Bengal for centuries.

The color of a bruise shifts from red to blue-purple to yellow-green as it heals — each stage reflects the chemical breakdown of hemoglobin into different compounds that absorb different wavelengths of light.

Chameleons do not primarily change color for camouflage — studies suggest the changes are mainly social signals, with males shifting toward brighter colors during territorial confrontations and duller tones when submitting.

The red color of autumn leaves is not present during summer — the anthocyanin pigments responsible for red and purple tones are synthesized fresh in the dying leaf, rather than being revealed by the breakdown of green chlorophyll.

NASA's Mars rovers confirmed that Martian sunsets appear blue rather than the orange-red of Earth's — fine Martian dust scatters shorter blue wavelengths inward toward the sun, the inverse of Earth's atmospheric scattering effect.

The word 'aquamarine' comes from the Latin for 'sea water' — ancient Roman sailors believed genuine aquamarine gemstones would protect them from drowning and carried them as talismans on ocean voyages.

Linguists classify ancient Egyptian as unusual among ancient languages for having a specific word for blue, developed alongside the invention of Egyptian Blue pigment — Homer's ancient Greek texts, by contrast, contain no word for blue at all.

Vermilion, one of the most prized red pigments in Renaissance painting, was made from ground cinnabar — a mercury sulfide mineral — and painters who used it extensively were at significant risk of chronic mercury poisoning.

Studies suggest that yellow-painted rooms cause infants to cry more frequently and couples to argue more often than rooms in other colors, possibly because high-saturation yellow increases physiological arousal.

The green ink formulation used on US currency is a proprietary compound developed by the Bureau of Engraving and Printing — the precise formula is classified specifically to complicate counterfeiting.

Studies show that blue-spectrum light from screens suppresses melatonin production significantly more than red-spectrum light at the same brightness, explaining why amber 'night mode' settings measurably improve sleep onset time.

The shade of vivid cherry red known as 'cerise' was popularized during the 1850s alongside synthetic aniline dyes — it briefly became so fashionable in Europe that French fashion journalists declared it 'the color of the decade.'

The bright orange of survival equipment — life vests, life rafts, and aviation emergency beacons — was standardized after tests confirmed orange offered the highest visibility contrast against both open ocean water and overcast sky.

The amber color of whiskey, bourbon, and aged rum comes entirely from the charred oak barrels used during aging — freshly distilled spirits are colorless, and unaged 'white whiskey' is sold completely clear.

'Millennial Pink' — the desaturated salmon-blush tone that dominated design and fashion from roughly 2014 to 2019 — was first identified as a cohesive named trend by researchers tracking Instagram hashtag patterns across design disciplines.
Studies suggest that elevator riders in warm-colored cabins rate their wait time as shorter than riders in cool-colored cabins, even when the ride duration is identical.
The cooked orange-red of shrimp and lobster is caused by astaxanthin, a carotenoid pigment masked by proteins in living crustaceans — heat denatures those proteins, releasing the bright color.

Operating theatre scrubs are green because surgeons focusing on red blood under bright lights experience red afterimages — wearing green, the complementary color, suppresses these ghost images and reduces eye strain.

The oldest known use of cosmetic red pigment — ochre mixtures found in shell containers in South Africa — is dated to approximately 100,000 BC, suggesting humans used red for personal decoration long before the development of cave art.
Research across 110 languages found a universal hierarchy in basic color terms: if a language has only two color words, they are always black and white; if there is a third, it is always red.

Studies suggest that people who have been completely blind since birth still consistently associate red with anger and danger, and blue with calmness — indicating some color-emotion associations may be encoded in language rather than visual experience.

Mountbatten Pink — a pinkish-grey developed for the British Royal Navy in World War II — was claimed by Lord Mountbatten to make ships less visible at dawn and dusk, though naval historians debate the evidence for its battlefield effectiveness.

Studies suggest that warm-colored restaurant interiors cause diners to eat faster, while cool blues and greens result in longer meals — a finding actively applied in the interior design of fast-food chains versus fine-dining establishments.
Red appears in approximately 77% of all national flags worldwide — more frequently than any other color, including blue or white.

The ancient Chinese word 'qing' historically covered both blue and green in the same way English once used 'blue' for the sea and sky with no distinction — the character still appears in modern Chinese in contexts where English speakers would clearly separate the two colors.

Sea glass collectors classify colors by rarity — green, brown, and clear are most common, from old bottles — while red and orange are among the rarest because relatively few glass products were manufactured in those colors historically.

Studies suggest that people rate wines in opaque or dark bottles as tasting superior to identical wine in clear glass — packaging color demonstrably alters flavor perception independent of price or label.

The pigment color 'Hooker's Green' is named after 19th-century English botanist Sir William Hooker, who developed a stable green pigment for precise botanical illustration — one of the few pigment names honoring a scientist rather than a place or material.

Coral bleaching occurs when warming water causes corals to expel the symbiotic algae called zooxanthellae living in their tissue — the white color of bleached coral is the exposed calcium carbonate skeleton.

Studies suggest that approximately 12% of women carry a genetic mutation giving them a fourth type of cone cell — a condition called tetrachromacy — which could theoretically allow them to distinguish vastly more shades than standard trichromatic vision.
Butterflies can see ultraviolet light, meaning flowers that appear plain white or yellow to humans display elaborate ultraviolet nectar patterns that are visible only to their pollinators.

Gold in heraldry is always rendered as yellow in two-dimensional work — the distinction between 'or' (heraldic gold) and plain yellow exists only in descriptive convention, not in the actual pigment applied to the surface.
The fuchsia flower was named after German botanist Leonhart Fuchs — whose surname means 'fox' in German — meaning the color fuchsia is ultimately named after a fox.

Studies suggest that sports referees unconsciously penalize athletes in black uniforms more frequently, associating the color with aggression — a finding first published using NFL and NHL game data in 1988.

Red lipstick has documented precedent across virtually every human civilization — Mesopotamian women created it from crushed gemstones as early as 5,000 years ago, while ancient Egyptian queens wore carmine derived from crushed beetles.
The British Royal Mail has used the same shade of red — sometimes called 'Post Office red,' close to Pantone 485C — for letter boxes since their national standardization in 1874.

Studies suggest that purple is consistently associated with luxury and mystery across cultures with no shared history, a pattern researchers attribute to the extreme historical rarity and expense of purple dye rather than any shared visual learning.

Studies suggest that people working in blue offices consistently estimate room temperature as approximately 3–4 degrees cooler than colleagues in red offices at the same actual temperature — a finding used in workplace ergonomics and building management.

The vivid blue of the Hope Diamond comes from trace boron atoms incorporated into the crystal lattice during formation — most diamonds contain nitrogen impurities instead, which makes them appear faintly yellow.

Schiaparelli Pink — the shocking magenta popularized by Italian-French designer Elsa Schiaparelli in 1937 — was considered transgressive in high fashion for a full decade before gradually entering mainstream use.

The deep black lacquer of traditional Japanese urushi ware is achieved through over 100 individually applied coats over several months — the optical darkness comes from extreme surface smoothness rather than from any uniquely dark pigment.
Studies suggest that food served under red light is perceived as more flavorful and aromatic than identical food under white light, an effect documented in controlled sensory evaluation environments.

The color indigo that dyes blue jeans was once grown as an agricultural crop across South Carolina and Bengal — synthetic indigo, perfected in 1897, collapsed the natural indigo market within a single generation.
